lists.official_name: Section 1260H of the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) for FY2021 — Chinese Military Companies
lists.authority: Sec. 1260H, William M. (Mac) Thornberry NDAA for FY2021 (Pub. L. 116-283), codified at 10 U.S.C. 113 note
lists.snapshot: Federal Register notice of June 10, 2026 (Vol. 91, No. 111)
lists.update_cadence: Updated at least annually until December 31, 2030
lists.description: The Department of War's annual list of "Chinese military companies" operating directly or indirectly in the United States. Companies are designated for ownership by or affiliation with the People's Liberation Army, China's defense ministries, or the military-civil fusion enterprise. Includes major technology, aerospace, shipping, and AI companies.
lists.researcher_note: Many companies on this list have commercial subsidiaries, U.S. research labs, or academic partnership programs. A collaboration, sponsored-research agreement, or gift relationship with any listed company or its employees would fall under the NSF prohibition.

lists.official_name: Section 1286 of the NDAA for FY2019, as amended — List of Foreign Talent Programs and Institutions
lists.authority: Sec. 1286, John S. McCain NDAA for FY2019 (Pub. L. 115-232), as amended by Sec. 1746, NDAA FY2020
lists.official_url_label: DoW Academic Research Security program page
lists.update_cadence: Updated periodically by the Office of the Under Secretary of Defense (Research & Engineering)
lists.description: The Department of War's list of foreign entities — mostly universities and research institutions in China, Russia, and other countries — identified as posing a risk to Department-funded fundamental research, including institutions connected to foreign military or malign foreign talent recruitment programs.
lists.researcher_note: This is the list most directly relevant to academic collaborations: it names universities. Co-authorship arrangements, joint projects, visiting appointments, or advising relationships involving these institutions on an NSF-funded project would fall under the prohibition.

lists.official_name: Entity List (Supplement No. 4 to Part 744 of the Export Administration Regulations)
lists.agency: U.S. Department of Commerce, Bureau of Industry and Security
lists.authority: 15 C.F.R. Part 744, Supplement No. 4 (Export Administration Regulations)
lists.update_cadence: Amended frequently — often several times per month — via Federal Register rules
lists.description: The largest and most frequently updated list here: foreign entities (companies, universities, research institutes, individuals) subject to export license requirements because of activities contrary to U.S. national security or foreign policy interests. Spans 80+ countries; includes many foreign universities.
lists.researcher_note: Because the Entity List changes so often, always re-check the live eCFR version or the Consolidated Screening List before relying on any snapshot — including this site's.

lists.agency: U.S. Department of Commerce, Bureau of Industry and Security
lists.authority: 15 C.F.R. Part 744, Supplement No. 7 (Export Administration Regulations)
lists.update_cadence: Amended periodically via Federal Register rules
lists.description: Foreign parties that BIS has determined are 'military end users' — entities that represent an unacceptable risk of use in, or diversion to, military end uses in China, Russia, Belarus, Burma, Cambodia, or Venezuela.
lists.researcher_note: MEU designations often cover aviation, aerospace, and technology firms whose civilian-facing divisions still count as restricted entities under the NSF prohibition.

lists.agency: U.S. Department of Commerce, Bureau of Industry and Security
lists.authority: 15 C.F.R. Part 764 (denial orders under the Export Administration Regulations)
lists.update_cadence: Updated as denial orders are issued, renewed, or expire
lists.description: Individuals and companies whose export privileges have been denied by BIS order, typically following export-control violations. Mostly individuals and small firms worldwide, each with a specific denial period.
lists.researcher_note: Denial orders have expiration dates — an entry here may have lapsed, and new orders appear regularly. Verify current status at the BIS site.

lists.official_name: Annex to Executive Order 14032 (Chinese Military-Industrial Complex Companies)
lists.agency: U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control
lists.update_cadence: The Annex itself is fixed; subsequent designations appear on the NS-CMIC List
lists.description: The annex of Chinese companies named in President Biden's 2021 executive order addressing the threat from securities investments that finance certain companies of the People's Republic of China — the foundation of the Chinese Military-Industrial Complex sanctions program.
lists.researcher_note: Companies from this annex generally also appear on the NS-CMIC List, which is the actively maintained version. Check both.

lists.official_name: Non-SDN Chinese Military-Industrial Complex Companies List
lists.agency: U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control
lists.authority: Executive Order 13959, as amended by Executive Order 14032
lists.update_cadence: Updated as OFAC adds or removes designations
lists.description: OFAC's actively maintained list of Chinese Military-Industrial Complex companies subject to U.S. investment prohibitions. Overlaps substantially with the E.O. 14032 Annex but includes later additions.
lists.researcher_note: Includes prominent technology and semiconductor firms. OFAC also offers a sanctions list search tool at sanctionssearch.ofac.treas.gov.

lists.agency: U.S. Department of State, Directorate of Defense Trade Controls
lists.authority: Sec. 38(g)(4) of the Arms Export Control Act; 22 C.F.R. 127.7 (ITAR)
lists.snapshot: DDTC statutory debarment list of August 25, 2025 (revised)
lists.update_cadence: Updated as debarments are imposed, rescinded, or reinstatements granted
lists.description: Persons convicted of violating or conspiring to violate the Arms Export Control Act, and consequently prohibited from participating in the export of defense articles and services. Mostly individuals.
lists.researcher_note: Debarred persons may later be reinstated — the DDTC page notes rescissions. Verify current status before drawing conclusions about a named individual.

lists.agency: U.S. Department of State, Directorate of Defense Trade Controls
lists.snapshot: DDTC administrative debarment list of June 1, 2023
lists.update_cadence: Updated as administrative debarments are imposed or lifted
lists.description: Persons debarred by DDTC administrative action (rather than criminal conviction) from participating in ITAR-regulated activities, typically pursuant to consent agreements or charging letters.
lists.researcher_note: This snapshot dates to mid-2023; always confirm on the DDTC site, which lists later actions and reinstatements.

lists.official_name: Nonproliferation Sanctions (Bureau of International Security and Nonproliferation)
lists.authority: Multiple statutes and executive orders, including INKSNA, the Iran, North Korea and Syria Nonproliferation Act, and E.O. 12938/13382
lists.update_cadence: Updated as determinations are published in the Federal Register
lists.description: Foreign individuals and entities sanctioned under U.S. nonproliferation statutes for transfers related to weapons of mass destruction or missile technology, spanning many countries and legal authorities.
lists.researcher_note: Sanctions under some statutes expire after a fixed term; a party can appear in the master table with lapsed measures. Check the effective and expiration dates in the official table.

lists.official_name: List of Equipment and Services Covered by Section 2 of the Secure Networks Act
lists.authority: Secure and Trusted Communications Networks Act of 2019, Sec. 2; 47 C.F.R. 1.50002
lists.update_cadence: Updated as the Public Safety and Homeland Security Bureau adds determinations
lists.description: Communications equipment and services deemed to pose an unacceptable risk to U.S. national security — naming producers such as Huawei, ZTE, Hytera, Hikvision, Dahua, Kaspersky, and Chinese state telecom carriers, including their subsidiaries and affiliates.
lists.researcher_note: The FCC list names companies through their equipment and services. Note that designations explicitly extend to subsidiaries and affiliates.

lists.official_name: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (UFLPA) Entity List
lists.authority: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act (Pub. L. 117-78), Sec. 2(d)(2)(B)
lists.update_cadence: Updated by the Forced Labor Enforcement Task Force, typically several times a year
lists.description: Entities in or connected to the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region identified for involvement in forced-labor practices — mining, manufacturing, labor-transfer programs, and procurement of affected goods.
lists.researcher_note: Listings span agriculture, polysilicon, apparel, and electronics supply chains. Collaboration risk most often arises through industry partners or materials suppliers rather than universities.

lists.official_name: Withhold Release Orders and Findings List
lists.update_cadence: Updated as orders are issued, modified, or revoked
lists.description: Import enforcement actions against specific producers, goods, or regions where CBP has information indicating forced-labor production. Entries date back decades; each has an Active or Revoked status.
lists.researcher_note: This list targets merchandise as well as named entities, and includes long-revoked historical orders — pay attention to the status shown with each result.
